Interdictor technology, particularly ship-based [[Gravity well/Legends|gravity wells]], gave rise to a spiral of advances and countermeasures over the course of thousands of years; the relatively weak Interdictors of the Mandalorian Wars were soon rendered ineffective by better hyperdrive [[Sensor/Legends|sensor]] suites and multiphase null field units, and the [[Clone Wars/Legends|Clone Wars]] almost four thousand years later inspired new research into the technology. [[Ion blaster/Legends|Ion blasters]] also saw increased use during and after Mandalorian Wars, as they proved effective in disabling the weaponry of the Mandalorian raiders.<ref name="Warfare" />

Thousands of years later,<ref name="NEC" /> in the era of the [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Galactic Empire]], scientist [[Bevel Lemelisk]] included the Mass Shadow Generator in a list of historical superweapons in ''[[Imperial Handbook: A Commander's Guide]]''. However, by that time the exact details of the weapon's effect on Malachor V were unknown, though the planet had long since been destroyed.<ref name="Hand">''[[Star Wars: Imperial Handbook: A Commander's Guide]]''</ref> As one of the defining conflicts of its era, the Mandalorian Wars was often referenced in scholarly texts and documents; the Jedi Master [[Gnost-Dural]] wrote about it briefly while chronicling his investigations into the Mandalorians of his time,<ref name="Gnost">[[The Journal of Master Gnost-Dural (real-life book)|''The Journal of Master Gnost-Dural'']]</ref> and the [[Historian/Legends|historian]] [[Vilnau Teupt]] spoke about the conflict during his speech "[[Industry, Honor, Savagery: Shaping the Mandalorian Soul]]" in [[24 ABY/Legends|24 ABY]].<ref name="Warfare" />

In the years after the [[Battle of Yavin]] in [[0 ABY]], a scholar named [[Ashur Sungazer]] on the planet [[Eriadu]] discovered the [[Ilum Talisman]], an artifact that was once worn by Master Isa and, unbeknownst to Sungazer, contained Isa's spirit that had persisted after her death. Sungazer recruited a number of adventurers to help him investigate the artifact, but drew the attention of [[Eren Garai]] of the [[Imperial Security Bureau/Legends|Imperial Security Bureau]]. One of the adventurers experienced a vision of Isa's finals moments upon holding the Talisman, and the group soon discovered the ''Sanctuary''{{'s}} wreckage in the Koler system along with an intact and partially-active Basilisk war droid.<ref name="Lessons" />

===The Mandalorians===
{{Quote|The war we had with the Republic was supposed to be the most glorious battle of our history but… it was a very costly one. I guess we didn't think of how much we could lose in it.|Canderous Ordo|<ref name="KOTOR" />}}
[[File:Canderousmandalorefull.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Mandalore the Preserver]]
In the weeks that followed the Battle of Malachor V—which became known as ''Ani'la Akaan'', or the Great Last Battle, in ''Mando'a''—Revan ordered that the remaining Mandalorians be stripped of their weapons and armor, and also that they dismantle their basilisk war droids. Knowing that they would only resume the fight if a new leader arose, Revan kept Mandalore's Mask, its loss greatly demoralizing the Mandalorians. Most of the warriors soon became mercenaries or [[Bounty/Legends|bounty]] hunters as the clans largely fragmented—without the Mask, the various clans fought amongst themselves for power. Over the following years, some Mandalorian clans, such as Clan Ordo, began searching for the Mask, identifying all of the possible worlds Revan could have reached in the three days during which he and Malak disappeared after Malachor. Less than fifty Mandalorians were part of the search on the first world, taking them two years, but as they moved from planet to planet, their numbers grew as more clans joined them. The search for the Mask united the Mandalorian clans behind a common cause, and by the time they started searching Rekkiad in [[3954 BBY]], over a hundred clans had joined the search.<ref name="Revan" />

Revan, who had lost his memories during the Jedi Civil War, sought the help of his friend Canderous Ordo—who had fought alongside Revan as one of his companions during the Jedi Civil War—to investigate his returning memories in 3954 BBY, and the two traveled to Rekkiad to join the search. With Revan's help, Clan Ordo discovered the Mask in Dramath's tomb, and Revan convinced Ordo to take up the Mask as Mandalore the Preserver. Before departing to retrace his and Malak's steps into the Unknown Regions, Revan asked the new Mandalore to restore the strength of the Mandalorians in order to protect the Republic from the mysterious Sith threat.<ref name="Revan" /> The Preserver spent the next few years reuniting the clans and rebuilding their military strength, establishing a headquarter on Dxun as he restored the Neo-Crusader movement. In the [[Dark Wars]] that followed the end of the Jedi Civil War, one of the new Sith Lords that rose to power was [[Darth Nihilus]], a survivor of the Battle of Malachor who was transformed by the experience, becoming a being who craved [[Force energy]], as well as a living wound in the Force.<ref name="KOTOR II" /><ref name="Revan" /> Nihilus pulled the [[Ravager (Centurion-class)|''Ravager'']], one of the ''Centurion''-class [[Battlecruiser/Legends|battlecruisers]] destroyed in the Battle of Malachor V, from the planet's surface using the Force and appropriated it as his own flagship. The ''Ravager'' was ultimately destroyed by both Republic and Mandalorian forces, with Mandalore the Preserver accompanying Meetra Surik—by then known as the Jedi Exile—in a final assault on the ship as his forces fought alongside the Republic.<ref name="KOTOR II" />

Despite the failure of the Mandalorian Wars, Mandalore the Ultimate's reforms had lasting effects on Mandalorian society—the Taung species became virtually extinct, with the clans becoming Human-dominated and accepting of those warriors who proved themselves worthy.<ref name="Warfare" /> In Mandalorian culture, a ritual known as the ''[[Geroya be Haran]]'', or "game of annihilation," developed in the [[Standard century/Legends|centuries]] following the Mandalorian Wars. The loser would have his entire legacy erased, and many believed that the tradition was a symbolic retelling of the Neo-Crusaders' greatest victories during the Mandalorian Wars.<ref name="Geroya">{{TORcite|type=codex|Geroya Be Haran}}</ref> However, Mandalore the Preserver was the last known Mandalore to wear Mandalore's Mask, which disappeared after the Preserver's death.<ref name="TORE" /> Many clans grew rich from plundering during the Mandalorian Wars, and lost those riches after the conflict ended, leaving them wishing for a return to war.<ref name="Ruhnuk">{{TORcite|exp=lots|type=general|Showdown on Ruhnuk|Ruhnuk}}</ref>

==Behind the scenes==
{{Quote|So there's war, and then there's war — and there's something like that going on here. People on Taris, near the frontier where #0 established that the Republic and Mandalorians have been quibbling inconsequentially for more than a year, feel they're at war. Regular folk on Coruscant? Not so much. What results is a more nuanced picture for those looking to put events into a simple timeline — as with a lot of Earthly wars, there's more than one starting date. There's when the Mandalorians started attacking non-Republic worlds; there's when the Republic intervened in one of those attacks; and, now, when the Mandalorians attacked the Republic in full force.|John Jackson Miller, in his production notes for issue 8 of the ''Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic'' comic series|<ref name="Miller 8">{{Faraway|url=comics/swknights/swkotor08.html|text=''Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic #8''}}</ref>}}
[[File:MandWars_Map.jpg|thumb|right|250px|''The Essential Atlas''{{'s}} map of the Mandalorian Wars]]
The Mandalorian Wars were first mentioned in the [[Star Wars video games|video game]] ''[[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic]]'', which was released by [[BioWare]] in [[2003]]. The conflict was created as backstory for several of the main characters in the game, such as Darth Malak, Revan, and Canderous Ordo,<ref name="KOTOR" /> and it was expanded upon in the game's [[2004]] sequel, ''[[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic II: The Sith Lords]]''.<ref name="KOTOR II" /> Other than material related to the video game, the conflict was not touched upon in many sources until the end of [[2005]], when it was discussed in ''[[The New Essential Chronology]]''.<ref name="NEC" /> The [[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic (comic series)|''Knights of the Old Republic'']] [[Star Wars comic books|comic book]] series, which started in [[2006]], was the first to visually depict the conflict; various battles appeared in different story arcs of the series, starting with the conflict's first appearance in [[Knights of the Old Republic 6|issue 6]].<ref name="KOTOR 6" /> [[John Jackson Miller]], the author of the series, used the comics to develop the Mandalorians as a fighting force, evolving them from the individualistic warriors who appeared in the [[Star Wars: Tales of the Jedi (comic series)|''Star Wars: Tales of the Jedi'']] comic series and the uniform, [[Color/Legends|color]]-coded soldiers who appeared in the ''Knights of the Old Republic'' video games.<ref name="Miller 26">{{Faraway|url=comics/swknights/swkotor26.html|text=''Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic #26''}}</ref>

The starting date of the conflict has varied between sources—the ''Knights of the Old Republic'' game itself gives two dates for the conflict's start, with the Mandalorian Wars beginning in 3976 BBY, according to the Mandalorians, and 3963 BBY, according to the Republic.<ref name="KOTOR" /> However, ''The New Essential Chronology'' and [[Star Wars: The Ultimate Visual Guide|''The Ultimate Visual Guide'']] respectively placed the endpoints at [[3995 BBY]]<ref name="NEC" /> and 3961 BBY.<ref name="Ultimate">''[[Star Wars: The Ultimate Visual Guide]]''</ref> The ''Knights of the Old Republic'' comic series reconciled the conflicting Mandalorian and Republic accounts of the start date, explaining that the Mandalorians had been raiding for years before the "False War" of 3965–3964 BBY, after which armed conflict broke out between the Republic and Mandalorians in 3963 BBY.<ref name="Miller 8" /> The ''[[Knights of the Old Republic Campaign Guide]]'', released in [[2008]], placed the Battle of Malachor V in 3960 BBY rather than 3961 BBY, and also included the False War in the main Mandalorian Wars by placing the start date as 3965 BBY. The ''Campaign Guide'' explained that the Mandalorians had been raiding Outer Rim worlds since 3976 BBY, though the Republic did not actually engage the invaders until 3965 BBY, and the true invasion of the Republic began at the end of 3964 BBY.<ref name="Campaign" /> The [[2009]] reference guide ''[[The Essential Atlas]]'' reaffirmed the ''Campaign Guide''{{'s}} dating and also gave a further breakdown of the main Mandalorian Wars, introducing a number of new battles on a map of the conflict.<ref name="Atlas" />

The three invasion corridors that the Mandalorians use during the Onslaught have been a subject of some confusion among various sources. ''The Essential Atlas'' features a map of the Mandalorian Wars, and the three invasion corridors are shown to be three campaigns spreading out from Taris, matching up with Canderous Ordo's statement that the Mandalorians invaded the Republic through three adjacent sectors.<ref name="Atlas" /> The [[2012]] reference guide ''[[The Essential Guide to Warfare]]'' identifies the corridors as the northern campaign, the Coreward campaign that includes Serroco, and the southern campaign that starts with Eres II.<ref name="Warfare" /> However, Eres is part of the Mandalorian Triumph, which does not begin until 3962 BBY.<ref name="Atlas" /> As ''Warfare''{{'s}} section on the conflict is presented as an [[in-universe]] oral presentation given thousands of years afterward, the account is not necessarily perfectly accurate.<ref name="Warfare" /> The ''Atlas''{{'s}} map also introduces a campaign on the eastern edge of the galaxy, set in 3963 BBY, that was not mentioned in any other source. However, the text indicates that those battles actually occurred beforehand, during the decade-long buildup to the main conflict,<ref name="Atlas" /> and the text is backed up by ''Warfare''<ref name="Warfare" /> and ''The New Essential Chronology''.<ref name="NEC" />

''[[The Complete Star Wars Encyclopedia]]'', published in 2008, mentions the conflict in a variety of entries,<ref name="Jaga" /><ref name="CSMW">{{CSWECite|II|285|Mandalorian Wars}}</ref> though there are some errors.<ref name="CSWE">''[[The Complete Star Wars Encyclopedia]]''</ref> Cassus Fett's entry erroneously states that the Battle of Jaga's Cluster occurred before the Mandalorian Wars, which is contradicted by the other entries and material in which the battle is mentioned.<ref name="Cassus">{{CSWECite|I|267|Fett, Cassus}}</ref> Another dating issue centers around when the Revanchists actively joined the Republic Military. According to [[Bastila Shan]]'s dialogue in the first video game, the Revanchists joined the Republic five years before the game, which is set in 3956 BBY—thereby placing the Revanchists' entrance into the war in 3961 BBY.<ref name="KOTOR" /> However, subsequent sources have instead placed the Revanchists' entry in 3963 BBY.<ref name="Campaign" /><ref name="KOTOR 42" /> ''The Essential Guide to Warfare'' attempted to reconcile the contradiction, explaining that Revan was appointed Supreme Commander of the Republic Military in 3962 BBY.<ref name="Warfare" />
